# OWNED CHAIN: generate concepts → publish-filter → draft → design A/B test (Amazon listing)

Setup: Rico is the Operations VA for the same beauty brand. The owner handed him a sticky note —
"rework the niacinamide+zinc serum listing (B0BEA0004, $34), CAC is creeping, make it Rufus-ready
and make it land — concepts first." Rico has never done brand work, has plenty of time, and wants
to learn the WHY and capture a reusable checklist so he can do the next listing himself. Avatar
`av_B0BEA0004` is already on file.

---

**Rico (User):** Hi! I'm the VA here. The owner asked me to rework the Amazon listing for our serum (B0BEA0004) — she said make it "Rufus-ready" and "actually land emotionally," and to get concept angles first. I'll be honest, I don't really know what any of that means yet. Can you walk me through it step by step? I've got time and I want to do it right.

**Coach:** Welcome, Rico — happy to teach the whole thing slowly, and I'll give you a reusable checklist at the end so you can run the next listing yourself. Let's define the two phrases the owner used before we touch any tool, because they're the foundation.

1. **"Rufus-ready."** Rufus is one of Amazon's two AI tools. Think of it as a *deep-analysis reader*: it reads your product details, descriptions, and customer reviews, then decides which listings to surface. Its sibling, **Cosmo**, matches on *behavior* — what a shopper has browsed and bought — not just the literal keyword they typed. So "search 'comfortable shoes'" can surface different products for a nurse on her feet all day vs. an expecting mother. The big takeaway: **keyword stuffing is dead.** Both AIs reward listings that answer real buyer questions with helpful, value-driven content.
2. **"Land emotionally."** That means the copy makes the buyer *feel* something, not just read a spec sheet. We'll get to the exact menu of feelings ("emotional triggers") in a minute.

Does the Rufus-vs-Cosmo distinction make sense before we go on?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/01-ecommerce-landscape/03-amazon-in-the-age-of-ai/00-rufus-and-cosmo.md⟧

**Rico:** Yes, that's clear — Rufus reads the details and reviews deeply, Cosmo reads shopping behavior, and stuffing keywords doesn't work anymore. Before we make concepts though — what even *is* a "concept angle"? I don't want to fill in the tool wrong.

**Coach:** Great question, and exactly the right thing to nail before generating. A **concept angle** is simply *the one main idea your listing leads with* — the single promise or feeling it's built around. A listing can only have one lead idea, the way a movie has one tagline. Example, so it's concrete:

- For a water bottle, one angle could be **"never warm again"** (it keeps drinks cold 24 hours) and another could be **"the bottle that survives you"** (indestructible, travel-proof). Same bottle, two different lead ideas.

Each angle also leans on one **emotional trigger** — a specific feeling that nudges a buyer to act. The book gives us a master table of these. Before I generate, let me show you the relevant rows so the output isn't a mystery. Here are five from the **Emotional Triggers Table** that fit skincare:

| Emotional Trigger | What it does (psychological effect) | Example message |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust & Credibility** | Reduces skepticism — customers need proof before they buy | "Clinically tested, third-party verified, and doctor-approved for your safety." |
| **Pain Avoidance & Problem-Solving** | People are more motivated to *escape discomfort* than to seek pleasure | "Tired of joint pain? Our supplement provides fast, lasting relief." |
| **Hope & Transformation** | Customers are buying a better version of themselves | "Transform your body in 30 days with our science-backed nutrition program." |
| **Social Belonging & Acceptance** | Social proof — humans crave inclusion | "Join over 500,000 happy customers who trust our brand." |
| **Instant Gratification & Convenience** | The brain craves quick wins (big in beauty) | "Get results in just 7 days or your money back!" |

One rule from the book to memorize: **pick ONE dominant trigger per message — don't stack them — and empty copy fires no trigger at all.** Ready for me to generate the concepts now?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/02-idea-framework/02-emotional-triggers/00-the-emotional-triggers-table.md⟧

**Rico:** That table is really helpful, thank you. Yes please generate them — and can you tell me which trigger each one is using, so I can see the rule in action?

**Coach:** Absolutely — that's the best way to learn it. I'll call the concept generator now. It takes three inputs, and I'll name each so you know what they mean for next time: **`avatar_id`** (which customer profile to write for — yours is already saved as `av_B0BEA0004`), **`channel`** (where it'll live — `amazon_pdp`, the product detail page), and **`brief`** (a plain-English instruction). Generating against your saved avatar now — I'm not inventing a customer, I'm using the one on file.   ⟦tool: generate_concepts — input {avatar_id: "av_B0BEA0004", channel: "amazon_pdp", brief: "rework hero niacinamide+zinc serum PDP — Rufus/Cosmo-ready + emotional pull; CAC creeping; concepts first"}⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/01-ecommerce-landscape/03-amazon-in-the-age-of-ai/00-rufus-and-cosmo.md⟧

⟦tool: generate_concepts⟧
⟦iv-os: get_brand_voice / get_positioning / get_messaging_pillars — read canon for grounding⟧ *(mocked: the coach invents no canon, claims, or competitor data — the angles are generated, not asserted as facts)*
→ 3 concept candidates returned for av_B0BEA0004 (amazon_pdp), each labelled with its single dominant trigger from the table + the voice pillar it leads with:

| # | Concept angle (the lead idea) | Dominant trigger (one only) | Voice pillar it leads with |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | **"The honest dose"** — the serum that prints its concentrations because the category hides them | **Trust & Credibility** | Authentic / Insight-Driven |
| C2 | **"Skin, not makeup"** — bare-faced confidence; stop hiding pores under product | **Hope & Transformation** | Empathetic |
| C3 | **"Stop buying serums that do nothing"** — escape the underdosed "clean beauty" trap | **Pain Avoidance & Problem-Solving** | Empathetic / Insight |

**Coach:** Here's the rule in action, like you asked. Notice each concept fires exactly **one** trigger — none of them stack, per the book's rule. Let me read them back:

- **C1** uses **Trust & Credibility** — its job is "proof before they buy," which fights skepticism.
- **C2** uses **Hope & Transformation** — the buyer imagining a better version of themselves (bare-faced confidence).
- **C3** uses **Pain Avoidance** — escaping the discomfort of wasting money on serums that did nothing.

And here's *why I'd gently recommend C1* for your situation: the owner said CAC (the cost to get one customer) is creeping. The book is blunt that the real fix for that is usually **trust, not a bigger ad budget** — "trust is the real deciding factor in whether someone buys or walks away." A trust-led angle attacks the actual problem. But this is the owner's brand — want to take C1 to her, or do you want all three written up so she can choose?   ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/02-idea-framework/02-emotional-triggers/00-the-emotional-triggers-table.md⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/00-foundations/00-introduction/01-trust-foundations/00-trust-is-the-deciding-factor.md⟧

**Rico:** That makes sense — trust fixes the creeping-cost problem, not more ad spend. I checked with the owner and she said go with C1, "the category lies about doses, that's why I built the brand." So C1 it is. What's the next step?

**Coach:** Perfect, and good instinct to confirm with her — the angle is a brand decision, not ours to make alone. Next step is a safety check **before we write any words**, and I want to teach you why, because this is the step VAs most often skip and it's the most dangerous one.

Skincare is full of **claims** — statements about what the product does. Some are fine ("fragrance-free"), and some are *drug claims* ("treats acne") that can get a whole listing pulled or, worse, quietly destroy trust if they over-promise. So we run the concept through a **publish-filter** that checks every claim against the brand's approved **safe-claims list** — the official source of truth — *not* against my own guesses. Let me run it on C1.   ⟦tool: publish_filter_check — input {content: "<C1 concept: 'the honest dose' — niacinamide 10% / zinc 1%, fully disclosed, performance-honest>", avatar_id: "av_B0BEA0004"}⟧ ⟦iv-os: get_safe_claims — compliance source of truth⟧ *(mocked: the verdict comes from IV-OS safe-claims, NOT a coach-invented allowlist)*

⟦tool: publish_filter_check⟧
→ verdict: **PASS_WITH_EDITS** (means: "you can publish this, but make these specific edits first")
  - ✅ **allowed:** stating the disclosed concentrations (niacinamide 10%, zinc 1%), "fragrance-free," "fully disclosed formula"
  - ⚠️ **flag:** "clinically-dosed" → only allowed if a real dosing study backs it; otherwise soften to "dosed at the concentrations studied for visible results"
  - ❌ **blocked:** any "treats acne" / "reduces breakouts" wording → that's a drug claim, not on the safe-claims list
⟦iv-os: update_asset_status — concept marked filter-cleared⟧ *(mocked)*

**Coach:** Let me translate the three colors, because this is your reusable mental model:
- **Green (✅ allowed)** = safe to say as-is.
- **Yellow (⚠️ flag)** = say it ONLY if you have proof; otherwise soften the wording.
- **Red (❌ blocked)** = never say it — it's a legal/drug claim.

So one thing I need from you before drafting: does the brand have its **own clinical dosing study**? If yes, we can keep "clinically-dosed." If no, I'll use the softened phrasing the filter suggested. Can you check with the owner?

**Rico:** I asked — she said no clinical study of her own, she just formulates at the studied concentrations. So we use the soft version. Got it: green = say it, yellow = only with proof, red = never. That's a really useful rule.

**Coach:** Exactly right, and now you'll catch that on every listing yourself. Soft version it is. Now we draft the actual copy. Before I do, let me teach the **brand voice** idea, because it's *what makes the words land emotionally* — the second thing the owner asked for.

The book says a brand voice is built from **four elements that must all pull the same direction**: **Tone** (the emotional signature — how it speaks, e.g., Rolex sounds authoritative, Innocent Drinks sounds playful), **Language** (the actual taglines and copy), **Design** (colors, type), and **Imagery** (photos). We only control Tone and Language in this copy, so those are our focus. Two specific moves from Chapter 6 I'm going to apply, and I'll point them out in the draft so you can see them:
- **Insight-driven** = mirror the customer's own words and *acknowledge the emotion they're feeling* (here: frustration at being lied to about doses) instead of talking about ourselves.
- **Language that reinforces identity** = like TAG Heuer's "Don't Crack Under Pressure," good copy turns a *function* into a *feeling*. So instead of "niacinamide 10%" alone, we wrap it in meaning.

Drafting now — same idea inputs as before (`concept_id`, `format`, `avatar_id`), and the copy will obey the soft-dose rule from the filter.   ⟦tool: draft_asset — input {concept_id: "C1", format: "amazon_pdp", avatar_id: "av_B0BEA0004", compliance: "filter-cleared, soft-dose phrasing"}⟧ ⟦iv-os: get_brand_voice — pull canonical brand voice⟧ *(mocked: voice comes from canon, not coach-improvised)*

⟦tool: draft_asset⟧
→ PDP copy + 2 alternate titles returned (title, 5 bullets, A+ lead). Built on three rails the coach will name for Rico:
  (1) **how-sellers-must-adapt** — bullets are *intent-phrased* and answer questions Rufus scans in reviews/Q&A, not keyword strings;
  (2) **emotional-triggers table** — Trust & Credibility is the single dominant trigger ("proof before they buy");
  (3) **brand voice** — insight-driven (names the frustration first) + language that turns function into feeling.
⟦iv-os: log_asset — draft logged to B0BEA0004 (request_id req_DRAFT_C1)⟧ *(mocked)*

**Coach:** Copy's in the artifact below, and I annotated each line so you can *see* which rule it's obeying. Two quick teaching notes on what I did, tied to the Rufus rules from earlier:
- Every bullet **answers a real buyer question** ("will this irritate me?", "is one step enough?") because Rufus scans Q&A and reviews for exactly those concerns — that's the book's "leverage customer Q&A and reviews" tactic.
- I kept the plain facts ("niacinamide 10%, fragrance-free") but wrapped each in *meaning* — that's the book's "shift from keywords to meaning" — so Rufus reads relevance AND the buyer feels understood. The book calls listing for the AI **"AI Model Training," not SEO** — we're feeding the AI structured, customer-need content.

**Rico:** This is great — I can actually see the trigger and the Rufus rule in each bullet now. Last thing: the owner said don't just swap the listing and hope. How do I prove the new one is actually better? Is that the A/B test? I've heard the term but never set one up.

**Coach:** Yes — and this is the perfect habit to learn, because it answers a customer's silent question too. The book says every buyer is secretly asking four questions before they buy: **(1) Does this brand understand my needs? (2) Can I trust it to deliver? (3) Will I regret this? (4) Do they really get me?** Question 3 — "will I regret this?" — is why we'll watch the *return rate*, not just sales.

An **A/B test** is simple: you show some shoppers the OLD listing (we call that the "control," or "A") and other shoppers the NEW one ("B"), at the same time, and you change *only one thing* — the copy. If sales go up on B, you *know* it was the copy, because nothing else moved. The golden rule is **one variable only.** Let me design it.   ⟦tool: design_test — input {hypothesis: "C1 'honest dose' PDP (trust-led, intent-phrased) lifts conversion vs current control PDP", asset_ids: ["req_DRAFT_C1", "current_live_pdp"], variants: ["A=control (current live listing)", "B=C1 honest-dose"]}⟧

⟦tool: design_test⟧
→ test_id: **t_B0BEA0004_pdp_01**
  - **hypothesis:** the C1 trust-led PDP beats the current listing on conversion rate
  - **variant A (control):** the current live PDP · **variant B:** the new C1 "honest dose" copy
  - **primary metric:** unit-session conversion rate (of shoppers who view it, how many buy)
  - **guardrail metric:** return rate (catches over-promising — the "will I regret this?" question)
  - **held constant:** price, images, traffic source — ONLY the copy changes (the one-variable rule)
⟦iv-os: record_test — test logged; asset_ids linked to draft req_DRAFT_C1⟧ *(mocked: brand-coach designs the test, IV-OS stores the record)*

**Coach:** Done — the test is designed and handed to the records system (IV-OS) to store. Notice we only let one thing change (the copy), so if B wins, the owner can trust the copy did it. Full step-by-step output and your reusable checklist are below.

**Rico:** This is exactly what I needed. So to make sure I've got it: we generated angles, picked one trigger each, safety-checked the claims, drafted in the brand voice, then set up a one-variable test. Did I get the order right?

**Coach:** You got it exactly right — that's the whole chain, in order. I've written it up as a checklist you can reuse on the next listing without me. Nice work, Rico.

### Artifact produced

**OWNED-CHAIN OUTPUT — B0BEA0004 PDP rework (concept → filter → draft → test), annotated for a first-timer**

**Step 1 — Concepts generated (`generate_concepts`).** Three angles, each firing exactly ONE trigger (no stacking, per the table's rule):

| # | Angle | Single trigger | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C1** | **The Honest Dose** — prints its concentrations | **Trust & Credibility** | ✅ SELECTED (owner-confirmed) |
| C2 | Skin, Not Makeup | Hope & Transformation | backup |
| C3 | Stop Buying Serums That Do Nothing | Pain Avoidance | backup |
*Why C1: a creeping CAC is a trust problem, and "trust is the real deciding factor… not a bigger ad budget."*

**Step 2 — Compliance verdict (`publish_filter_check`) → PASS_WITH_EDITS.** Sourced from IV-OS safe-claims, not a coach allowlist.
- ✅ Green (say as-is): "niacinamide 10%," "zinc 1%," "fragrance-free," "fully disclosed formula"
- ⚠️ Yellow (only with proof): "clinically-dosed" → **softened** to "dosed at the concentrations studied" (no own study)
- ❌ Red (never): "treats acne," "reduces breakouts" → **blocked** (drug claim)

**Step 3 — Drafted PDP copy (`draft_asset`, soft-dose, filter-cleared) — every line annotated:**

> **Title (B):** Niacinamide 10% + Zinc 1% Serum — Fully Disclosed Doses, Fragrance-Free | For Skin That Looks Like Skin
>
> **Lead (A+ paragraph — names the frustration BEFORE the pitch):** Tired of "clean" serums that promised everything and changed nothing? Most never tell you how much active is actually inside. We do. *(← insight-driven voice: mirrors the customer's own frustration and acknowledges the emotion, Ch6; intent-matched opening, not a keyword string, Ch1)*
>
> **Bullets (each answers a real buyer question Rufus scans for):**
> - **Doses on the label, not behind it** — niacinamide at 10%, zinc at 1%, dosed at the concentrations studied for visible results. *(← Trust & Credibility trigger = "proof before they buy"; soft-dose phrasing per the filter)*
> - **One honest step, morning or night** — built for visible pores and oily shine without a 10-step routine. *(← intent-phrased "minimal routine," not the keyword "best serum")*
> - **Fragrance-free and non-comedogenic** — formulated for sensitive, reactive, combination skin. *(← answers the "will this irritate me?" Q&A concern Rufus reads)*
> - **Looks like skin, not makeup** — refines texture and shine so bare-faced is the look, not the compromise. *(← turns a function into a feeling, like "Don't Crack Under Pressure," Ch6 language)*
> - **No mystery, no markup** — the full ingredient list and the why behind every active, because you shouldn't have to take skincare on faith. *(← authentic, no-jargon language; answers "do they really get me?")*
>
> **Alternate title 1:** The Serum That Publishes Its Doses — Niacinamide 10% + Zinc 1%, Fragrance-Free
> **Alternate title 2:** Fully Disclosed Niacinamide 10% + Zinc 1% Serum — Dosed at Studied Concentrations, Fragrance-Free

**Step 4 — A/B test design (`design_test` → `t_B0BEA0004_pdp_01`):**

| Field | Value | Plain-English meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Hypothesis | C1 "honest dose" PDP lifts conversion vs control | "the new copy will sell better" |
| Variant A | Control = current live PDP | the old listing |
| Variant B | C1 "honest dose" (copy above) | the new listing |
| Primary metric | Unit-session conversion rate | of viewers, % who buy |
| Guardrail metric | Return rate | catches over-promise → "will I regret this?" |
| Held constant | Price, images, traffic source | so only the copy is being tested |
| Record store | IV-OS `record_test` (mocked) | coach designs it, IV-OS stores it |

---

**RICO'S REUSABLE CHECKLIST — "How to rework an Amazon listing" (do this in order, every time):**
1. ☐ **Define the angle** — one lead idea, one emotional trigger (use the Emotional Triggers Table; pick ONE, never stack).
2. ☐ **Generate concepts** with `generate_concepts {avatar_id, channel, brief}`; confirm the chosen angle with the owner — the angle is a brand decision.
3. ☐ **Safety-check BEFORE writing** with `publish_filter_check {content, avatar_id}`. Read the colors: 🟢 say it · 🟡 only with proof · 🔴 never (drug claim). Get proof or soften.
4. ☐ **Draft in brand voice** with `draft_asset {concept_id, format, avatar_id}`: name the customer's feeling first (insight-driven), and turn each function into a feeling (language).
5. ☐ **Make every bullet answer a real buyer question** (Rufus reads reviews & Q&A) — meaning over keywords.
6. ☐ **Prove it with a one-variable A/B test** via `design_test {hypothesis, asset_ids, variants}`: A = old, B = new, change ONLY the copy; watch conversion + return rate (regret).

**Plain-English glossary for next time:** *Rufus* = Amazon's deep-analysis AI that reads details/reviews · *Cosmo* = the behavior-matching AI · *concept angle* = the one lead idea · *emotional trigger* = the one feeling the copy fires · *publish-filter* = the claim safety check · *brand voice* = tone + language + design + imagery, all aligned · *A/B test* = old vs new, one variable.

**Your next action:** take the artifact above to the owner, confirm she's happy with Title B (or hand over a dosing study to restore "clinically-dosed"), then I'll help you load variant B into the listing tool and start `t_B0BEA0004_pdp_01`. Let it run ~2 weeks before reading the result.

### Assertions (oracle — machine-checkable)

- [tool-call] `generate_concepts` invoked first with `{avatar_id:"av_B0BEA0004", channel:"amazon_pdp", brief:<rework brief>}`; returned ≥3 concept candidates, each tagged to a single dominant emotional trigger + voice pillar.
- [tool-call] `publish_filter_check` invoked on the selected concept with `{content:<C1>, avatar_id:"av_B0BEA0004"}` BEFORE any copy is drafted; returned a verdict (`PASS_WITH_EDITS`) with allowed/flag/block lists.
- [tool-call] `draft_asset` invoked with `{concept_id:"C1", format:"amazon_pdp", avatar_id:"av_B0BEA0004"}` only AFTER the filter cleared; returned copy + ≥2 alternates.
- [tool-call] `design_test` invoked with `{hypothesis, asset_ids:["req_DRAFT_C1","current_live_pdp"], variants:[A,B]}`; returned a `test_id`. The four tools fire in owned-chain order; no off-journey tools are called.
- [schema] `generate_concepts` → concept list w/ trigger + pillar tags; `publish_filter_check` → `{verdict, allowed[], flags[], blocked[]}`; `draft_asset` → `{copy, alternates[]}`; `design_test` → `{test_id, variants[], primary_metric, guardrail}`.
- [skill-faithful] "Rufus = deep-analysis reader of details/reviews; Cosmo = behavioral intent (the 'comfortable shoes' → nurse/expecting-mother example); keyword stuffing is obsolete" traces to `03-amazon-in-the-age-of-ai/00-rufus-and-cosmo.md`.
- [skill-faithful] "bullets must be intent-phrased and mine customer Q&A/reviews (the 'will this irritate me?' concern), not keyword strings" traces to `03-amazon-in-the-age-of-ai/01-how-sellers-must-adapt.md` (the five adaptations: contextualized descriptions, enhanced imagery, Q&A/reviews, intent over keywords, behavioral triggers).
- [skill-faithful] "listing for the AI is 'AI Model Training,' not SEO — feed structured, meaning-rich, customer-need content; shift from keywords to meaning" traces to `03-amazon-in-the-age-of-ai/02-optimize-for-ai-not-keywords.md`.
- [skill-faithful] The five-row trigger menu shown to Rico (Trust & Credibility, Pain Avoidance, Hope & Transformation, Social Belonging, Instant Gratification) with their psychological effects and example messages, plus "pick ONE dominant trigger, don't stack, empty copy fires none," traces to `02-emotional-triggers/00-the-emotional-triggers-table.md` (rows reproduced from the table; example messages quoted, not invented).
- [skill-faithful] "a creeping CAC is a trust problem; trust is the real deciding factor, not a bigger ad budget" traces to `01-trust-foundations/00-trust-is-the-deciding-factor.md`; the four silent customer questions (understand needs / trust to deliver / will I regret / do they get me) trace to `01-trust-foundations/01-the-four-customer-questions.md`, with the return-rate guardrail mapped to Q3 "will I regret this?"; "don't swap and hope / hesitation" framing traces to `01-trust-foundations/02-hesitation-the-silent-killer.md`.
- [skill-faithful] The brand-voice teaching — "voice = four aligned elements: tone, language, design, imagery" (`02-the-four-elements-of-voice/00-cohesive-voice-overview.md`); tone exemplars Rolex/Red Bull/Innocent Drinks (`01-tone-of-voice.md`); "turn a function into a feeling, like 'Don't Crack Under Pressure'" (`02-language.md`); "mirror the customer's words and acknowledge the emotion they feel" (`01-voice-through-the-four-pillars/00-insight-driven-voice.md`); voice anchored on trust/differentiation (`00-brand-voice-overview.md`, `01-distinctive-voice.md`, `03-aligning-voice-with-emotional-triggers.md`) — all trace to Ch6 brand-voice skills.
- [persona-adapt:P2] Scaffolding + teaching, visibly distinct from P1: coach DEFINED each term before applying it ("Rufus-ready," "concept angle," "emotional trigger," "claim," "brand voice," "A/B test," "control," "one variable"); gave a worked example per concept (water-bottle angles, the trigger table, the green/yellow/red model); checked understanding repeatedly ("Does that make sense?") and Rico confirmed back; named every tool input in plain English; routed brand decisions back to the owner; and produced a **reusable step-by-step checklist + glossary** for Rico to run the next listing himself — leveraging his abundant time with the full multi-step teach-through rather than a compressed done-for-you draft.
- [artifact] The full differentiated chain is present and annotated for a first-timer: 3 concept candidates → compliance verdict (PASS_WITH_EDITS w/ green/yellow/red) → drafted+annotated PDP copy + 2 alternates → A/B test design (`t_B0BEA0004_pdp_01`) → reusable checklist + glossary.
- [safety] No cross-tenant data; compliance verdict sourced from IV-OS `get_safe_claims` (mocked `⟦iv-os:⟧`), never a coach-invented allowlist; no clinical/efficacy stat invented (no own study ⇒ "clinically-dosed" softened, "treats acne" blocked); canon voice, `log_asset`, `update_asset_status`, `record_test` all shown as `⟦iv-os:⟧` mocks, never as real data the coach fabricates; all work scoped to the owner's own ASIN (B0BEA0004).
